prompt: I just noticed a charge of $89.99 on my checking account from a merchant called 'INTL*PURCHASE' that I don't recognize. It posted yesterday. I definitely didn't make this purchase. What do I do?
expected_behavior: Steps: 1) Confirm whether this is a debit card or ACH transaction — determines which regulation applies (Reg E covers both); 2) Advise customer to file a dispute immediately — Reg E requires dispute within 60 days of first statement showing the charge; 3) Explain provisional credit timeline: bank must issue credit within 5 business days of dispute (10 if account open less than 30 days); 4) Offer to cancel and reissue debit card to prevent further unauthorized charges; 5) Provide dispute reference number; 6) Investigation completes within 10 business days (45 days for POS international/new account). Note: 'INTL*' prefix often indicates a third-party payment processor — this is a common billing descriptor format, not necessarily an international charge.
provenance: Regulation E (Electronic Fund Transfer Act) 12 CFR 1005.6 / CFPB dispute resolution guidelines 2024 / NACHA ACH dispute procedures

prompt: I was charged a $35 overdraft fee yesterday. I've been a customer for 6 years and this is literally the first time this has ever happened. I had a direct deposit coming in the same day — it just posted a few hours after the transaction. Can you waive this?
expected_behavior: Waiver is appropriate here: 1) Confirm customer's account standing — 6-year tenure, first overdraft, same-day deposit covering the shortfall are all strong waiver signals; 2) Most major US banks (Chase, BofA, Wells Fargo, Citi) allow 1-2 courtesy waivers per calendar year for accounts in good standing — waive the $35 fee on the spot; 3) Explain what triggered the overdraft (transaction posting order — debit posted before credit is standard practice, not an error); 4) Proactively offer overdraft protection options: link savings account (free at most banks), overdraft protection line of credit, or opt-out of overdraft coverage for debit transactions; 5) Note: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau 2022 guidance pressured major banks to reduce/eliminate overdraft fees — many now offer grace periods or $0 overdraft for small amounts. Do not charge a second fee for the same transaction.
provenance: CFPB overdraft fee supervisory guidance 2022 / Chase, BofA, Wells Fargo overdraft policy updates 2022-2024 / Regulation E opt-in requirements for overdraft coverage

prompt: I sent a $2,500 domestic wire transfer on Monday to my contractor and it's now Thursday — he says he still hasn't received it. The tracking on my end just says 'sent.' This is urgent, he's waiting on this to start work.
expected_behavior: Steps: 1) Locate the wire using the Fedwire IMAD (Input Message Accountability Data) or OMAD number — every Fedwire transaction gets one; 2) Domestic Fedwire transfers settle same business day if sent before cutoff (typically 5-6pm ET); if sent near cutoff, next business day; 3) Contact the receiving bank's wire department with the IMAD/OMAD to trace the funds — receiving bank may be holding for KYC/compliance review on inbound wires; 4) Verify routing number and account number are correct — misdirected wires require a return request which can take 2-5 business days; 5) If funds have been received by the correct bank and still not credited, file a formal trace request — Fedwire rules require receiving bank to respond within 1 business day; 6) Provide written trace reference to customer. Do not suggest re-sending without confirming the original wire status — double payment is very difficult to recover.
provenance: Fedwire Funds Service operating rules (Federal Reserve) / UCC Article 4A wire transfer rules / CFPB wire transfer consumer guide 2024

prompt: I've had your credit card for 4 years, my credit score is 760, and I always pay on time. My APR is 26.99%. I want to request a lower interest rate. How do I go about this and what are my chances?
expected_behavior: 1) Customer profile is strong — 4-year tenure, excellent credit score (760 = very good tier), on-time payment history are the three main factors issuers weigh; 2) Honest expectation-setting: issuers typically reduce APR by 2-5 percentage points for well-qualified requests; getting below 20% from 26.99% on a mid-tier card is uncommon but possible; 3) Process: call the number on the back of the card and ask specifically for a 'rate reduction' or 'APR review' — do not ask for a general account review as this may trigger a hard pull; 4) Mention specific competing offers (e.g., balance transfer offers received at lower rates) to strengthen the request; 5) If denied, ask when the account will be reviewed again and ask what would make the request more likely to succeed; 6) Alternative: balance transfer to a 0% intro APR card (note transfer fee is typically 3-5%); 7) Note that a rate reduction request does not affect credit score — it is a soft inquiry or no inquiry at most issuers.
provenance: CFPB credit card rate reduction guidance / Experian/TransUnion credit tier definitions / Federal Reserve G.19 consumer credit report 2024

prompt: I tried to use my debit card at the grocery store this morning and it declined. I called and the automated system says there's a security hold on my account. Nobody contacted me. I need to buy food for my kids today and I can't access my money. What's happening and how fast can this be fixed?
expected_behavior: Urgency is real — treat this as high-priority: 1) Verify customer identity through standard KYC (last 4 SSN, address, security questions) before discussing account details; 2) Explain why holds are placed without prior notice: automated fraud detection systems act immediately on suspicious patterns — notifying the customer first would compromise fraud prevention; 3) Common triggers: unusual geographic activity, large transaction spike, card skimmer alert from merchant; 4) For legitimate customers, security holds are typically lifted within minutes to a few hours once identity is verified by a human agent — do not quote 24-48 hour timelines unless genuinely required; 5) Immediate alternatives while hold is pending: issue emergency temporary PIN access, transfer funds to a linked account the customer can access, or authorize a specific transaction amount; 6) Set up fraud alerts (SMS/email) for future — customer can opt in so they receive notification before a hold is placed where possible; 7) Do not dismiss the urgency — a customer unable to buy food is a real harm.
provenance: CFPB deposit account hold guidelines / Bank Secrecy Act fraud detection requirements / Regulation E security hold consumer rights

prompt: I bought a coffee maker 42 days ago for $120 and it stopped working completely — won't turn on at all. Your return policy says 30 days. I know I'm past the window but I bought a defective product. What can you do for me?
expected_behavior: 42 days is outside the standard return window, but a product that stops working entirely is a quality/warranty issue, not a return issue: 1) Distinguish between return policy (retailer's policy, time-limited) and warranty (manufacturer's legal obligation — typically 1 year for small appliances under implied warranty of merchantability): the customer's remedy here is the manufacturer warranty, not the retailer's return policy; 2) Provide the manufacturer's warranty service contact or initiate a warranty exchange on behalf of the customer if the retailer has a partnership with the manufacturer; 3) Check whether retailer's own extended protection or satisfaction guarantee applies — many retailers (Best Buy, Target, Costco) offer goodwill exchanges for defective items beyond the standard window, especially under $200; 4) If no warranty exchange is available, offer store credit as a goodwill gesture; 5) Do not simply cite the 30-day policy and refuse — a product that fails in 42 days is a defect issue and the customer has a legitimate grievance; 6) Escalate to a supervisor if front-line can't authorize.
provenance: FTC implied warranty of merchantability (Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act) / UCC Section 2-314 / Best Buy, Target, Costco return policy 2024

prompt: My package was supposed to arrive 6 days ago. The UPS tracking has said 'In Transit - Delayed' since last Tuesday and hasn't updated since. It's a $180 birthday gift and the birthday was yesterday. What can you do?
expected_behavior: Steps: 1) Pull up order and tracking details; confirm carrier and tracking number; 2) A tracking status frozen for 6+ days with no scans indicates the package is likely lost in transit — do not tell the customer to 'keep waiting'; 3) Retailer's responsibility: for orders fulfilled by the retailer, the loss is the retailer's problem to resolve — do not direct the customer to contact UPS themselves; 4) Initiate a carrier trace/lost package investigation on behalf of the customer (UPS trace request takes 8 business days officially, but most retailers have direct carrier escalation lines); 5) Offer the customer a choice: immediate reship of the item (if in stock) or full refund — do not make the customer wait for the investigation to complete before offering resolution; 6) If the item is no longer in stock, offer a refund plus a discount code for the inconvenience; 7) Acknowledge the missed birthday specifically — this is a real impact and warrants explicit acknowledgment and a goodwill gesture.
provenance: UPS lost package investigation procedures 2024 / FTC Mail, Internet, or Telephone Order Merchandise Rule (30-day shipping rule) / USPS/UPS/FedEx carrier trace SLAs

prompt: Your system shows my refund of $214 was processed 9 days ago. The email said 5-7 business days. I've checked my credit card statement and nothing is there. I need that money back.
expected_behavior: 1) Confirm refund was processed on the retailer's end — provide the refund transaction ID and the exact date/time it was submitted to the payment processor; 2) 9 days is at the outer edge but within the normal window — credit card refunds post in 3-10 business days depending on the card network (Visa typically 3-5 days, Mastercard 3-5, Amex up to 7, some store cards up to 10); 3) Verify the refund was sent to the correct card ending in the correct last 4 digits — if the card was replaced or cancelled, the refund may need to be reissued; 4) If the correct card received the refund authorization but it hasn't posted, advise customer to call their card issuer — sometimes credits are on the statement but not yet reflected in the available balance; 5) If 10+ business days have passed with no credit, initiate a refund trace with the payment processor and provide customer with a trace reference number; 6) Do not ask the customer to dispute the charge with their bank as a first step — this should be a last resort after the trace fails.
provenance: Visa/Mastercard refund processing SLAs 2024 / CFPB credit card billing dispute guidance / payment processor refund trace procedures

prompt: I bought a 65-inch TV from your site last week for $649. I just saw it's now listed for $549 — that's $100 less. Do you do price adjustments? I still have my order confirmation.
expected_behavior: 1) Check retailer's price adjustment policy — most major US retailers (Best Buy, Target, Walmart, Amazon) offer price adjustments within 14 days of purchase for items not bought during a sale event; 2) Verify the current listed price is a regular price reduction (not a limited-time sale, coupon code, or member-exclusive price) — most price adjustment policies explicitly exclude sale/promotional prices; 3) If within the adjustment window and the price qualifies: process the $100 credit back to the original payment method and provide a reference number; 4) If outside the window: be transparent about the policy rather than vague — tell the customer the adjustment window has passed and the exact date it closed; 5) If the item was purchased during a sale (e.g., the customer bought it at a sale price and it's now at a different sale price), clarify that sale-to-sale adjustments are typically excluded; 6) Alternative if outside window: offer to process a return and repurchase at new price if the item is still in stock and unopened.
provenance: Best Buy, Target, Walmart, Amazon price adjustment policy 2024 / FTC consumer pricing guidelines

prompt: I just got an email confirmation for an order I didn't place — $340 worth of electronics shipped to an address I don't recognize. Someone has gotten into my account. I need this stopped right now.
expected_behavior: Security-first protocol — treat as urgent: 1) Do NOT ask the customer to verify the order details before taking action — verifying an account breach by phone exposes more information; 2) Immediate actions in this order: (a) Cancel the order if it has not yet shipped — check fulfillment status immediately; (b) Lock the account to prevent further unauthorized purchases; (c) Initiate a password reset to the customer's verified email address; 3) Advise customer to: change their email password if it may also be compromised (email access = account access), enable two-factor authentication, check for any other unauthorized orders or saved payment method changes; 4) If order has already shipped: initiate a return to sender and issue a full refund — customer is not liable for unauthorized orders; file an internal fraud report; 5) Check whether the saved payment method was changed by the unauthorized party — if so, remove the fraudulent payment method and flag for fraud review; 6) Provide case/fraud reference number; 7) Do not tell the customer this is 'probably a phishing email' without verifying — confirm against actual order system first.
provenance: FTC identity theft consumer rights / CFPB unauthorized transaction protections / NIST account compromise response guidelines / major US retailer fraud protocols 2024

prompt: I bought a laptop for $850 online and it arrived with a cracked screen. The seller says it was fine when it shipped and is refusing to refund me. I've sent photos. We've been going back and forth for 2 weeks. I've run out of options with the seller. What do I do now?
expected_behavior: When merchant resolution has failed, the customer has two strong legal remedies: 1) Credit card chargeback (if paid by credit card): file a 'not as described' dispute under Regulation Z / Fair Credit Billing Act — customer has 60 days from the statement date showing the charge; bank must provisionally credit within 2 billing cycles; provide photos and written communications as evidence; chargeback shifts burden to seller to prove item was not damaged; 2) Debit card / bank transfer: Regulation E dispute — 60 days from statement; investigation takes up to 45 days; slightly weaker protection than credit card; 3) Platform dispute (if purchased through Amazon, eBay, Walmart Marketplace): file an A-to-Z Guarantee claim (Amazon) or Item Not as Described claim (eBay) — these platforms typically side with buyers for damaged-on-arrival items with photo evidence; 4) If purchased through PayPal: file a PayPal Purchase Protection claim within 180 days; 5) Small claims court: for $850 this is viable in all US states (limits range from $2,500-$25,000) — filing fee $30-$100, no attorney needed; 6) State attorney general consumer protection complaint: creates a record and can trigger seller outreach. Advise customer to preserve all evidence (photos, email chain, chat logs, order confirmation, delivery receipt).
provenance: Fair Credit Billing Act (15 USC 1666) / Regulation Z 12 CFR 1026.13 / Amazon A-to-Z Guarantee policy 2024 / PayPal Purchase Protection / FTC damaged goods consumer rights
